rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE NESTEL REVOCABLE TRUST, ESTABLISHED FEBRUARY 21, 1997 AND REVOKED ON JULY 1, 2019 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Order To Show Cause Re Preliminary Injunction (Added For The Purpose Of Posting Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Part 2 of 3 The record also shows that the balance of hardships tips in favor of Jane. There is a strong public policy in favor of enforcement of support orders and non-payment is presumed prejudicial. Jane has demonstrated that she would likely be harmed if the preliminary injunction is not granted and would be more harmed than the Trustee or William would likely be if the preliminary injunction is granted. It appears that under the 1999 Stipulation and Order Jane may be due substantial support payments-payments that have gone unpaid for many, many years. It is also apparent that if trustee Simon makes a distribution to William the support debt will continue to go unsatisfied; William's own evidence is that he will put the entirety of any distribution toward his own medical and support expenses, and he will pay nothing to Jane (see, e.g., Obj. at par. 28, "Billy will require all of the amount in Trust for his benefit for his support including his basic needs, housing, in-home caregivers, utilities, transportation, physical therapy, food and medical needs.") The court rejects as without meaningful factual support Jane's claim that William would "flee the country" if he is given his distributive share before her Petition is heard. There is no showing that the Trustee would be harmed by the preliminary injunction. The court acknowledges William's claim that he would be harmed by the preliminary injunction because he would not be able to invest those funds and use them to support his medical and care needs. Much of this delay, however, is attributable to his own decision to wait more than two decades to do anything about the 1999 Stipulation and Order. Moreover, any delay in distribution caused by this order is temporary. In any event, the court finds that the balance of hardships tips in Jane's favor. The court rejects William's claim that the requested injunction is overbroad because Jane's claim is substantially less than the total trust distributions due to William. On this record, the court cannot calculate the total trust distributions due William, in large part because many trust liabilities are currently uncertain or unsettled. The court also rejects William's Probate Code arguments. William has not demonstrated that the trust "provides that a beneficiary's interest in income is not subject to voluntary or involuntary transfer" or that any contemplated distribution is subject to such spendthrift clause and the rules set out in Chapter 2, Part 2 of Division 9 ("Chapter 2"). (See Prob. C., section 15300.) Even if Chapter 2 applies to the contemplated distributions, the court is not convinced it could decline to enforce a portion or all of the support judgment, absent competing judgments. (See Prob. C., section 15305.) Even if William's construction of Section 15305 is correct, on this record, the court cannot say that it will decline to enforce all or a portion of the support judgment, especially given the strong public policy in favor of satisfaction of support judgments. Finally, Section 15306.5's 25% percent cap does not apply to enforcement of support judgments. (See Prob. C., section 15306.5; see Cal. Law Rev. Com., com., Hirsch & McGovern Cal. Probate Code Ann. (2020 ed.) foll. section 15306.5, p. 1001 ["It should also be noted that while a spouse, former spouse, or minor child enforcing a support judgment may use [Section 15306.5], in the normal course, support creditors will apply under Section 15303. The limitations provided in [Section 15306.5] do not apply to enforcement of a support judgment under Section 15305."].) Part 2 of 3

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE NESTEL REVOCABLE TRUST, ESTABLISHED FEBRUARY 21, 1997 AND REVOKED ON JULY 1, 2019 TRUST
rulings.Rulings: Part 1 of 3 This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Joseph M. Quinn. Pending is Jane Terry Nestel's ("Jane") Petition for Enforcement of Money Judgment Against Beneficiary's Trust Interest in which she seeks to enforce a spousal support judgment against trust beneficiary William Nestel ("William") under a 1999 Stipulation and Order. Essentially, Jane wants Barbara Simon, as trustee of the Nestel Revocable Trust to satisfy the judgment by directing to her a substantial portion of the distribution due to William. Jane secured a TRO enjoining Simon from making any distributions to William without court order. Now before the court is the request for a preliminary injunction to the same effect. William filed an Objection to the Order to Show Cause Re: Preliminary Injunction. Jane filed a Reply to the Objection. Trustee Simon did not file a response, opposition, or objection to either the Order to Show Cause, the Respondent's Objection, or the Petitioner's Reply to Objection. Jane's request for a preliminary injunction is GRANTED. "A superior court must evaluate two interrelated factors when ruling on a request for a preliminary injunction: (1) the likelihood that the plaintiff will prevail on the merits at trial and (2) the interim harm that the plaintiff would be likely to sustain if the injunction were denied as compared to the harm the defendant would be likely to suffer if the preliminary injunction were issued." (Smith v. Adventist Health System/West (2010) 182 Cal.App.4th 729, 749.) The grant or denial of a preliminary injunction "does not amount to an adjudication of the ultimate rights in a controversy. It merely determines that the court, balancing the respective equities of the parties, concludes that, pending a trial on the merits," exercise of the right claimed by the defendant should or should not be restrained. (Jamison v. Department of Transp. (2016) 4 Cal.App.5th 356, 361.) Injunctions are equitable in nature and the general purpose of prejudgment injunctive relief is to preserve the status quo pending a determination on the merits. (Continental Baking Co. v. Katz (1968) 68 Cal.2d 512, 528; SB liberty, LLC v. Isla Verde Ass'n, Inc. (2013) 217 Cal.App.4th 272, 280.) The moving party bears the burden to establish all elements necessary to support issuance of a preliminary injunction. (See O'Connell v. Sup. Ct. (Valenzuela) (2006) 141 Cal.App.4th 1452, 1481.) Jane has met her burden. Jane has established that she is more likely than not to prevail on the merits of her Petition. The record demonstrates that the 1999 Stipulation and Order is outstanding and there is no material showing that the spousal support obligation has been satisfied. Although it is possible that the Los Angeles Superior Court, the issuing court, will reduce or eliminate William's outstanding spousal support obligation, status quo is the existence of the order and the unpaid spousal support. Part 1 of 3

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE NESTEL REVOCABLE TRUST, ESTABLISHED FEBRUARY 21, 1997 AND REVOKED ON JULY 1, 2019 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Order To Show Cause Re Preliminary Injunction (Added For The Purpose Of Posting Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Part 3 of 3 The court ORDERS Barbara Simon as Trustee of the Nestel Revocable Trust is restrained and enjoined from making any distribution or payment to or on behalf of Respondent William C. Nestel from the subject Trust without further order of this Court. Jane SHALL POST A BOND of $100,000 in connection with this order. Per CCP section 549(a) and CRC 3.1150(f), the court issuing a preliminary injunction must require the moving party to post an undertaking or bond in the amount the restrained party would be injured if the court later determines the injunction was wrongful. The bond amount is the court's best estimate on this record of the amount Simon as trustee could be injured as the restrained party. Jane shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. Remote appearance is available in conformity with the current General Order of the Probate and Mental Health Division, available on the court's website at https://sfsuperiorcourt.org/divisions/probate. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925 and passcode 722809. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925 and passcode 722809. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204. Part 3 of 3
